entities.description: A modeling entity for the wider first-, second-, and third-recension manuscript tradition Macaulay describes and collates. It prevents the Fairfax running copytext from being mistaken for the sole source of his eclectic readings without requiring every manuscript witness to be modeled as a separate facsimile target in this record.
entities.label: Macaulay's aggregate Confessio Amantis manuscript-collation record
notes: Macaulay reports full collation of Bodley 902, Corpus Christi College Oxford 67, and Bodley 294 alongside Fairfax 3 and selective checking of at least fourteen further witnesses wherever variation appeared.
This is a description of an editorial source set, not a physical manuscript or lost intermediary.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Late fourteenth to early fifteenth century

entities.description: A late-fourteenth- to early-fifteenth-century English parchment codex containing Confessio Amantis and other poems. Confessio occupies fols. 2r-186r and represents the third-recension text used by Macaulay as his running copytext. The manuscript is not an autograph and includes one main late-fourteenth-century hand and two early-fifteenth-century interventions.

holding.notes: Confessio Amantis is cataloged at fols. 2r-186r.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Fairfax 3

notes: Fols. 2 and 185 are replacement leaves. The main scribe writes through f. 185v apart from interventions; a later hand writes the first replacement leaf and fols. 186-194.
F. 186r is a shared boundary surface: Confessio ends there with its explicit and associated Latin verse; Traitié begins on f. 186v.

rights.basis: The work was composed and circulated in manuscript in the late fourteenth century, centuries before modern copyright.
rights.component: Underlying medieval text of Confessio Amantis

reviewNotes: The primary derivation chain is repository XML -> U-M CME -> OTA 1677 -> OTA 0063 -> Macaulay EETS English Works volumes I-II -> Fairfax 3 as running copytext, with Macaulay's wider collation represented separately.

The repository XML's source note says Complete Works volumes 3-4, but Confessio actually occupies Complete Works volumes II-III; the OTA record identifies the separately issued EETS English Works volumes I-II as the immediate source.

Fairfax 3 is a material base witness, not the sole source of Macaulay's eclectic apparatus. Macaulay says the running text follows Fairfax and every deviation is noted while numerous witnesses were collated.

The conventional three-recension vocabulary is preserved as an editorial classification with caveats: Macaulay himself warns that the so-called second recension is not a simple chronological middle stage.

The complete facsimile cache contains the whole codex. The work mapping was corrected from the acquisition command's provisional f. 186v/canvas 380 endpoint to the official catalog and visually verified f. 186r/canvas 379 endpoint; f. 186v begins Traitié.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus XML is a pinned repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item Confessio. Its 33,444 numbered Middle English lines descend through U-M's 1993 encoding from Oxford Text Archive U-1677-C/current item 1677, an SGML-tagged version of OTA 0063. OTA 0063 identifies its immediate source as G. C. Macaulay's two-volume EETS English Works of John Gower (1900-1901), Extra Series 81-82. Macaulay used Bodleian Library MS. Fairfax 3 as the running copytext for the third-recension text and collated it against a much wider manuscript tradition. The U-M/OTA stream omits the first- and second-recension passages, Latin verses and glosses, apparatus, notes, and print paratext. A complete 408-canvas color facsimile of Fairfax 3 and complete scans of both EETS source volumes and the parallel Clarendon Complete Works volumes II-III are cached with exact work mappings. Rights are recorded by layer because the medieval text and Macaulay edition are public domain in the United States, while OTA encodings, U-M assertions, Google scan terms, the repository Unlicense, and Digital Bodleian images carry distinct or conflicting statements.

catalogSummary.author: John Gower
catalogSummary.date: Composed c. 1386-1390 and revised through c. 1392-1393
catalogSummary.editor: G. C. Macaulay (editor of the 1900-1901 source edition)
catalogSummary.region: England; the exact place of composition is not established in this record
catalogSummary.title: Confessio Amantis

catalogingSubject.label: Confessio Amantis
catalogingSubject.scope_note: John Gower's late-fourteenth-century English literary work in a prologue and eight books, including its embedded Latin verses, headings, and glosses and its authorial revision history. This record describes the intellectual work, not Macaulay's 1900-1901 critical edition, the EETS or Clarendon publication packages, Fairfax 3 as a physical codex, the OTA/U-M encodings, or the repository XML. The present digital representation transmits only the English numbered running text of Macaulay's Fairfax-based third-recension edition.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Gower announces a book balancing pleasure and instruction, surveys division and corruption in church, polity, and the social estates, and uses the dream of Nebuchadnezzar's divided image to diagnose the fractured world.

contentParts.label: Book I: Pride
contentParts.summary: Amans encounters Venus and Genius and begins his confession. Genius examines forms of Pride as they operate in love and illustrates them with moral narratives.

contentParts.label: Book II: Envy
contentParts.summary: The confession turns to Envy and its branches, including sorrow at another's good, detraction, false semblance, and supplantation, with tales testing rivalry, speech, and loyalty.

contentParts.label: Book III: Wrath
contentParts.summary: Genius questions Amans about Wrath and its forms, using exempla of anger, contention, homicide, war, and patience to redirect destructive passion.

contentParts.label: Book IV: Sloth
contentParts.summary: The examination of Sloth treats delay, negligence, forgetfulness, idleness, and despair in love, countering them with stories of labor, fidelity, and purposeful action.

contentParts.label: Book V: Avarice

locators.value: vol. I ends at 5.1970; vol. II begins at 5.1971

contentParts.summary: The longest book examines Avarice and related forms of possession and exchange through an exceptionally broad sequence of tales and digressions, including accounts of religions, idolatry, astronomy, and alchemy.

contentParts.label: Book VI: Gluttony
contentParts.summary: Genius examines Gluttony, especially drunkenness and excess, and uses stories of appetite, intoxication, and self-command to test the lover's conduct.

contentParts.label: Book VII: The Education of a King
contentParts.summary: Departing from the direct sequence of sins, Genius recounts Aristotle's instruction of Alexander in the knowledge and virtues required for kingship, joining moral philosophy, political counsel, and advice for princes.

contentParts.label: Book VIII: Lechery and the Conclusion
contentParts.summary: The final book treats Lechery, especially incest, through major narratives including Apollonius of Tyre. Venus finally confronts Amans with his age, cures and releases him, and the poem closes in prayer for good governance and lasting joy.

notes: The opening date is approximate; the first form is conventionally associated with 1390.

scope.description: Initial composition through the first-recension completion

notes: The repository XML's flat 1390-1393 creation date corresponds more closely to the revision interval than to the entire period of composition.

scope.description: Additions, rearrangement, and revised dedication/epilogue states conventionally grouped as later recensions

extent.description: The repository representation contains a Prologue and Books I-VIII in 33,444 numbered Middle English verse lines: 1,088 + 3,446 + 3,530 + 2,774 + 3,712 + 7,844 + 2,440 + 5,438 + 3,172. Its 339 DIV2 units are supplied electronic 100-line groups, not authorial stanzas. The count excludes Latin verses/glosses, first- and second-recension variant passages, apparatus, and print paratext omitted upstream.

formStatements.description: A long narrative poem, predominantly in octosyllabic Middle English couplets. The form is cataloged as verse because all nine primary textual divisions are verse; embedded Latin prose glosses are integral paratext rather than alternating prose sections, so they do not make the work mixed in this schema.

genres.label: Frame narrative
notes: Amans's confession to Genius supplies the frame for moral instruction and a large collection of tales.

genres.label: Dream vision
notes: The work incorporates dream-vision conventions and visionary political imagery; the confessional dialogue and tale collection are equally central.

genres.label: Romance
notes: Romance applies to embedded narratives, not uniformly to every section of the whole work.

scope.description: Selected embedded tales, including Apollonius of Tyre

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The intellectual work and Macaulay edition contain Latin; the OTA/U-M/repository text selection omits it.

scope.description: Authorial Latin verses, headings, explicit material, and marginal gloss tradition accompanying the English poem

notes: The source-edition publication dates are 1900-1901 and Fairfax 3's physical production date is late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; neither should be substituted for Gower's multi-stage composition/revision dates.

The repository XML header's 'Complete Works' volumes 3-4 statement is bibliographically wrong. Confessio occupies Complete Works volumes II-III, while OTA 0063 identifies the separately numbered EETS English Works volumes I-II as the immediate source.
Macaulay's edition is eclectic in apparatus and editorial intervention even though Fairfax 3 is its explicit running copytext.
The tags describe the whole work and scoped major components, not every embedded tale; romance is intentionally scoped to selected narratives.
IMEV 2662 is independently confirmed by the University of Nottingham's WLC/LM/8 manuscript description; the earlier draft's 2262 was a transcription error.

The verse form classification describes the primary literary divisions. Integral Latin prose glosses remain recorded as embedded language/paratext and do not create prose content sections in the present schema.
The complete public Fairfax 3 facsimile is linked through the lineage manifest and cached outside git; the metadata record uses folio locators but does not treat the image files as the work itself.

next_steps: Consult current biographical and textual scholarship before assigning London, Southwark, or another locality as place of composition.
notes: England is secure as cultural origin; exact composition locality is intentionally not inferred from Gower's biography.
openQuestions.question: Can a precise place of composition within England be established for the work or for particular revision stages?

next_steps: Review current textual scholarship on recension terminology and manuscript groupings.
Map added, deleted, rearranged, and rewritten passages against witnesses before creating version records.
Keep the current record's whole-work description distinct from the repository artifact's narrower third-recension English selection.
openQuestions.question: Should future metadata model the first-, so-called second-, and third-recension states as related work versions with passage-level boundaries?

notes: Gower's Prologue says he writes a book 'for Engelondes sake'; this record does not convert that cultural association into a precise place-of-composition claim.
The three-recension terminology is a scholarly model of multiple textual states; it should not be read as three wholly separate works or a mechanically linear chronology.

scope.description: Authorial rewriting and additions reflected in the conventional recension groups

notes: This is representation-level editorial responsibility, distinct from Gower's authorship and medieval revision.

scope.description: The 1900-1901 printed source edition represented by the digital artifact

subjects.label: Seven deadly sins
notes: Book VII's education-of-a-king material interrupts the otherwise dominant sequence of sins; Book VIII treats Lechery.

subjects.label: Classical, biblical, and medieval narrative traditions

scope.description: The large body of embedded moral tales throughout the eight books

summaries.value: Confessio Amantis frames a wide-ranging collection of classical, biblical, and medieval tales as the confession of Amans, an aging lover, to Genius, priest of Venus. Organized largely around the seven deadly sins as they bear on love, the dialogue repeatedly broadens into ethics, social criticism, political counsel, natural lore, and narrative exempla. The Prologue surveys division in church, polity, and society; Book VII turns at length to the education of a king; and Book VIII concludes the treatment of lechery and releases the lover from his service to Venus.

The work survives in multiple authorial and manuscript states conventionally called first, second, and third recensions. Macaulay's running text follows the third-recension Fairfax 3 manuscript while recording departures and other-recension material in apparatus; OTA 0063, OTA 1677, U-M, and the repository XML strip that apparatus and retain only the 33,444 numbered English lines of the Fairfax-based third-recension stream.

notes: A functional English translation of the Latin title, not a separate medieval title witness.
Catalog/encoding title, not a claim that the possessive wording is authorial.

notes: First encoded English line, Prologue 1.
titles.value: Of hem that writen ous tofore

notes: Final encoded English line, Book VIII 3172; the Fairfax surface also carries a Latin explicit and associated verse omitted from the repository XML.
